title: Built to Break: The Hidden Economics of Things That Last
summary: An essay arguing that durability is quietly treated as an economic threat, and that the true carbon cost of a product is largely hidden in its making, not its use — with uncomfortable implications for how we measure 'green'.
body:
On a Saturday morning in a Berlin community centre, a retired electrical engineer named Ingrid bends over a toaster that stopped working three years ago. Around her, a dozen strangers wait with kettles, lamps, and a sewing machine inherited from a grandmother. This is a repair café, one of thousands that have sprung up from Amsterdam to Kyoto, and its quiet premise is mildly subversive: that a broken object is not necessarily rubbish. Ingrid prises open the toaster, finds a snapped plastic clip that costs almost nothing, and revives a machine the manufacturer would have been delighted to see replaced.

That last clause holds the essay's first uncomfortable truth. For a great many companies, a product that lasts is not a triumph but a problem. If a washing machine runs faithfully for twenty years, its maker sells one machine in twenty years; if it falters at eight, they sell two or three. Durability, viewed coldly through the ledger, is a threat to revenue — and the suspicion that some goods are engineered to fail just outside the warranty has a name, planned obsolescence, and a longer history than most people assume. It is tempting to cast this as simple villainy, but the reality is more diffuse and therefore harder to fix.

The diffuseness matters. Few engineers sit down to design failure on purpose. Rather, a thousand small decisions — gluing a battery in rather than letting it be swapped, choosing a cheaper bearing, withholding spare parts, releasing software that mysteriously slows older devices — accumulate into a system that rewards the short-lived. No single villain is required when the incentives all lean the same way. What looks like conspiracy is more often the unremarkable arithmetic of a market that prices the next sale and ignores the cost of the last one.

And here is the second, larger truth, the one that should unsettle anyone who believes they are buying their way to a greener life. The carbon footprint of most manufactured things is dominated not by how we use them but by how they are made. The mining, smelting, moulding, and shipping that bring a smartphone into existence — its embodied energy — typically dwarf the electricity it will sip over its working life. For such goods, the single most consequential green act is not switching them off more diligently; it is keeping them longer. A phone kept for five years is, in carbon terms, roughly twice as virtuous as the same phone replaced every two, however efficient the newer model claims to be.

This inverts a comfortable story we like to tell. We are encouraged to upgrade in the name of efficiency: the new fridge uses less power, the new car emits less, so surely replacing the old one is the responsible choice. Sometimes it genuinely is — for a device used constantly, where running emissions dominate, early replacement can pay its carbon debt. But for a great many possessions the debt is never repaid, because the bulk of the harm was done in the factory, before the thing was ever switched on. The price tag, which records only the moment of purchase, says nothing of this. It is silent about the river dammed, the ore extracted, the furnace fired half a world away.

None of this is an argument for hoarding broken junk or refusing all that is new; that would be a caricature. The point is narrower and sharper. A culture that measures environmental virtue chiefly at the point of use is measuring the wrong end of the object's life. Were we to price the embodied cost honestly — to make the maker, not merely the user, accountable for the carbon already spent — the case for repairability would cease to be a hobbyist's eccentricity and become plain economics. Some governments have begun to grope towards this: France now requires a repairability score on electronics, and the European Union has edged towards a 'right to repair'. These are first steps on a long road.

Ingrid, meanwhile, snaps the toaster shut and hands it back. The owner is delighted; the manufacturer, had it known, would have shrugged at a sale it never made. What the repair café offers is not nostalgia but a quiet correction to our accounting — a reminder that the greenest object is very often the one you already own, and that the cost most worth counting is the one no price tag ever shows.

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences for emphasis ('What looks like… is…')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ (cleft) như 'What looks like conspiracy is more often the unremarkable arithmetic…' tách một thành phần ra đầu câu để nhấn mạnh tương phản. Đây là dấu hiệu điển hình của văn nghị luận trình độ C1.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Cleft constructions like 'What looks like conspiracy is more often the unremarkable arithmetic…' front one element for contrastive emphasis. They are a hallmark of nuanced, argumentative C1 prose.

grammarNotes.title: Inverted conditional with 'Were we to…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Were we to price the embodied cost honestly… the case for repairability would cease to be…' là câu điều kiện loại hai đảo ngữ (bỏ 'if'). Cách viết trang trọng, giả định.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Were we to price the embodied cost honestly…' is an inverted second conditional that omits 'if' and fronts 'were', a formal, hypothetical structure typical of essay register.

grammarNotes.title: Concession then refinement ('None of this is… that would be…')
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Tác giả nhượng bộ ('None of this is an argument for hoarding… that would be a caricature') để loại trừ cách hiểu cực đoan trước khi nêu luận điểm chính xác hơn — kỹ thuật rào đón (hedging).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The concessive move 'None of this is an argument for hoarding broken junk… that would be a caricature' rules out an extreme reading before stating the sharper claim — a hedging technique central to C1 argument.

translationVi:
Vào một sáng thứ Bảy tại một trung tâm cộng đồng ở Berlin, một kỹ sư điện đã nghỉ hưu tên Ingrid cúi xuống bên một chiếc máy nướng bánh mì đã ngừng hoạt động ba năm trước. Quanh bà, cả tá người lạ chờ đợi với những ấm đun nước, đèn, và một chiếc máy may thừa kế từ bà ngoại. Đây là một quán sửa chữa (repair café), một trong hàng nghìn quán đã mọc lên từ Amsterdam đến Kyoto, và tiền đề lặng lẽ của nó hơi mang tính phá cách: rằng một vật bị hỏng không nhất thiết là rác. Ingrid cạy mở chiếc máy nướng ra, tìm thấy một cái kẹp nhựa bị gãy gần như chẳng đáng giá gì, và hồi sinh một cỗ máy mà nhà sản xuất hẳn đã rất vui khi thấy nó bị thay thế.

Mệnh đề cuối ấy chứa đựng sự thật khó chịu đầu tiên của bài luận. Với rất nhiều công ty, một sản phẩm bền không phải là thắng lợi mà là một vấn đề. Nếu một chiếc máy giặt chạy trung thành suốt hai mươi năm, nhà sản xuất bán được một chiếc trong hai mươi năm; nếu nó trục trặc ở năm thứ tám, họ bán được hai hoặc ba chiếc. Độ bền, nhìn một cách lạnh lùng qua sổ sách kế toán, là một mối đe dọa với doanh thu — và mối nghi ngờ rằng một số hàng hóa được thiết kế để hỏng ngay sau khi hết bảo hành có một cái tên, sự lỗi thời được lên kế hoạch, và một lịch sử dài hơn hầu hết mọi người tưởng. Thật dễ quy chụp điều này thành sự gian ác đơn thuần, nhưng thực tế tản mác hơn và do đó khó khắc phục hơn.

Sự tản mác ấy rất quan trọng. Ít kỹ sư nào ngồi xuống để cố tình thiết kế cho hỏng. Đúng hơn, cả nghìn quyết định nhỏ — dán cứng pin vào thay vì cho phép thay pin, chọn một ổ bi rẻ hơn, không cung cấp phụ tùng thay thế, tung ra phần mềm làm chậm một cách bí ẩn các thiết bị cũ — tích tụ thành một hệ thống tưởng thưởng cho thứ chóng hỏng. Không cần một kẻ xấu duy nhất nào khi mọi động cơ đều nghiêng về cùng một hướng. Cái trông như âm mưu thường chỉ là phép tính tầm thường của một thị trường định giá cho lần bán tiếp theo và phớt lờ chi phí của lần bán trước.

Và đây là sự thật thứ hai, lớn hơn, điều lẽ ra phải khiến bất kỳ ai tin rằng mình đang mua sắm để có một cuộc sống xanh hơn phải bất an. Dấu chân carbon của hầu hết các vật phẩm sản xuất bị chi phối không phải bởi cách ta dùng chúng mà bởi cách chúng được tạo ra. Việc khai mỏ, luyện kim, đúc khuôn và vận chuyển để đưa một chiếc điện thoại thông minh vào tồn tại — năng lượng hàm chứa của nó — thường lấn át lượng điện mà nó sẽ tiêu thụ trong suốt đời làm việc. Với những hàng hóa như vậy, hành động xanh có ý nghĩa nhất không phải là tắt chúng đi chuyên cần hơn; mà là giữ chúng lâu hơn. Một chiếc điện thoại được giữ năm năm, xét về carbon, đức hạnh gấp khoảng hai lần so với cùng chiếc điện thoại đó được thay mới hai năm một lần, dù mẫu mới có tuyên bố hiệu quả đến đâu.

Điều này đảo ngược một câu chuyện dễ chịu mà ta thích kể. Ta được khuyến khích nâng cấp nhân danh hiệu quả: tủ lạnh mới tốn ít điện hơn, xe mới phát thải ít hơn, nên chắc chắn thay cái cũ là lựa chọn có trách nhiệm. Đôi khi đúng là vậy thật — với một thiết bị dùng liên tục, nơi phát thải khi vận hành chiếm phần lớn, việc thay sớm có thể trả được món nợ carbon. Nhưng với rất nhiều vật sở hữu, món nợ ấy chẳng bao giờ được trả, bởi phần lớn tác hại đã gây ra ở nhà máy, trước khi vật ấy được bật lên lần nào. Cái nhãn giá, vốn chỉ ghi lại khoảnh khắc mua hàng, chẳng nói gì về điều này. Nó im lặng về con sông bị chặn đập, quặng bị khai thác, lò luyện được nhóm lửa ở nửa kia thế giới.

Không điều nào trong số này là một lý lẽ ủng hộ việc tích trữ đồ hỏng vô dụng hay khước từ mọi thứ mới; đó sẽ là một sự biếm họa. Luận điểm hẹp hơn và sắc hơn. Một nền văn hóa đo lường đức hạnh môi trường chủ yếu tại thời điểm sử dụng là đang đo đạc sai đầu của vòng đời sản phẩm. Nếu chúng ta định giá chi phí hàm chứa một cách trung thực — để buộc nhà sản xuất, chứ không chỉ người dùng, chịu trách nhiệm về lượng carbon đã tiêu — thì lý lẽ cho khả năng sửa chữa sẽ thôi còn là thú vui lập dị của dân nghiệp dư và trở thành kinh tế học thuần túy. Một số chính phủ đã bắt đầu lần mò tiến tới điều này: Pháp nay yêu cầu một điểm số về khả năng sửa chữa trên các thiết bị điện tử, và Liên minh châu Âu đã nhích tới một 'quyền được sửa chữa'. Đó là những bước đầu tiên trên một con đường dài.

Trong khi đó, Ingrid đóng sập chiếc máy nướng lại và trao nó về. Người chủ rất vui; nhà sản xuất, nếu biết, hẳn đã nhún vai trước một lần bán mà nó không bao giờ thực hiện được. Cái mà quán sửa chữa mang lại không phải là sự hoài niệm mà là một sự điều chỉnh lặng lẽ cho cách tính toán của chúng ta — một lời nhắc rằng vật xanh nhất thường lại chính là vật bạn đã sở hữu, và rằng chi phí đáng tính nhất lại là chi phí mà không nhãn giá nào từng thể hiện.

questions.prompt: What is the 'quiet premise' of the repair café described in the opening?
options: That broken objects should always be recycled into raw materials
That a broken object is not necessarily rubbish
That manufacturers should give refunds for faulty goods
That old appliances are more stylish than new ones
questions.explanation: The text states the premise is 'that a broken object is not necessarily rubbish'.
questions.hint: First paragraph describes the café's premise.

questions.prompt: Why would the toaster's manufacturer have been 'delighted' to see it replaced?
options: Because the toaster was dangerous to use
Because the design had been recalled
questions.explanation: The essay's first truth is that for many companies a lasting product is 'a problem' because it reduces repeat sales.
questions.hint: See the washing-machine arithmetic in the second paragraph.

questions.prompt: How does the writer characterise durability 'viewed coldly through the ledger'?
questions.explanation: Durability 'is a threat to revenue' when seen purely financially.

questions.prompt: What term does the writer give to engineering goods to fail just after the warranty?
questions.explanation: The suspicion 'has a name, planned obsolescence'.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer say the problem is 'harder to fix' than simple villainy?
options: Because it is diffuse, arising from many small decisions, not one villain
Because consumers refuse to repair anything
Because the technology is too advanced to understand
questions.explanation: The third paragraph argues 'No single villain is required when the incentives all lean the same way.'

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That engineers usually sit down deliberately to design failure on purpose
That gluing in batteries and withholding spare parts shorten product life
That incentives can produce short-lived products without a conspiracy
That a market may price the next sale and ignore the cost of the last
questions.explanation: The text explicitly says 'Few engineers sit down to design failure on purpose' — the opposite of option A.
questions.hint: Re-read the start of the third paragraph carefully.

questions.prompt: According to the essay, what dominates the carbon footprint of most manufactured things?
options: The electricity used during their working life
How they are made — their embodied energy
The packaging they are sold in
The fuel used to drive to the shop
questions.explanation: The footprint is 'dominated not by how we use them but by how they are made'.

questions.prompt: What does the writer call the single most consequential green act for such goods?
options: Buying the most efficient new model
questions.explanation: 'The single most consequential green act… is keeping them longer.'
questions.hint: Fourth paragraph contrasts switching off with keeping.

questions.prompt: What 'comfortable story' does the essay say is inverted?
options: That repairing is cheaper than buying new
That upgrading in the name of efficiency is the responsible choice
That community centres are good for neighbourhoods
That toasters are simple to fix
questions.explanation: The essay inverts the story that 'replacing the old one is the responsible choice' in the name of efficiency.
questions.hint: Fifth paragraph begins 'This inverts a comfortable story'.

questions.prompt: When does the writer concede that early replacement CAN be justified?
options: For a device used constantly, where running emissions dominate
questions.explanation: 'For a device used constantly, where running emissions dominate, early replacement can pay its carbon debt.'
questions.hint: Fifth paragraph offers a careful concession.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say the price tag is 'silent' about?
options: The river dammed, the ore extracted, the furnace fired
questions.explanation: The price tag 'is silent about the river dammed, the ore extracted, the furnace fired half a world away'.
questions.hint: End of the fifth paragraph lists hidden costs.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'measuring the wrong end of the object's life'?
options: Judging virtue at the point of use rather than at manufacture
Estimating the product's price too low
questions.explanation: A culture measuring virtue 'chiefly at the point of use is measuring the wrong end of the object's life' — ignoring manufacture.
questions.hint: Sixth paragraph sharpens the central claim.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say would happen 'were we to price the embodied cost honestly'?
options: The case for repairability would become plain economics
Carbon would no longer be measured
questions.explanation: Honest pricing would make 'the case for repairability… plain economics' rather than 'a hobbyist's eccentricity'.
questions.hint: Look for the inverted conditional in the sixth paragraph.

questions.prompt: Which concrete policy step does the essay cite as a 'first step'?
options: A tax on owning old appliances
questions.explanation: 'France now requires a repairability score on electronics', cited as a first step.
questions.hint: Sixth paragraph names a country and the EU.

questions.prompt: What is the essay's closing claim about the 'greenest object'?
options: It is the newest, most efficient model available
It is very often the one you already own
It is whatever can be recycled fastest
It is the cheapest one on the shelf
questions.explanation: The closing reminder is that 'the greenest object is very often the one you already own'.
questions.hint: The final sentence states it directly.

questions.prompt: What does the repair café ultimately represent, in the writer's view?
options: A quiet correction to our accounting of environmental cost
A way to save money on electricity bills
questions.explanation: It offers 'not nostalgia but a quiet correction to our accounting'.
questions.hint: The last paragraph reframes the café.

cloze.template:
On a Saturday morning in a Berlin community centre, a retired electrical engineer named Ingrid bends over a toaster that stopped working three years ago. Around her, a dozen strangers wait with kettles, lamps, and a sewing machine inherited from a grandmother. This is a repair café, one of thousands that have sprung up from Amsterdam to Kyoto, and its quiet premise is mildly {{b1}}: that a broken object is not necessarily {{b2}}. Ingrid prises open the toaster, finds a snapped plastic clip that costs almost nothing, and revives a machine the manufacturer would have been delighted to see {{b3}}.

That last clause holds the essay's first uncomfortable {{b4}}. For a great many companies, a product that lasts is not a triumph but a {{b5}}. If a washing machine runs faithfully for twenty years, its maker sells one machine in twenty years; if it falters at eight, they sell two or three. Durability, viewed coldly through the ledger, is a threat to {{b6}} — and the suspicion that some goods are engineered to fail just outside the warranty has a name, planned {{b7}}, and a longer history than most people assume. It is tempting to cast this as simple villainy, but the reality is more {{b8}} and therefore harder to fix.

The diffuseness matters. Few engineers sit down to design failure on {{b9}}. Rather, a thousand small decisions — gluing a battery in rather than letting it be swapped, choosing a cheaper bearing, withholding spare parts, releasing software that mysteriously slows older devices — accumulate into a system that rewards the short-lived. No single {{b10}} is required when the incentives all lean the same way. What looks like conspiracy is more often the unremarkable {{b11}} of a market that prices the next sale and ignores the cost of the last one.

And here is the second, larger truth, the one that should unsettle anyone who believes they are buying their way to a greener life. The carbon footprint of most manufactured things is dominated not by how we use them but by how they are {{b12}}. The mining, smelting, moulding, and shipping that bring a smartphone into existence — its embodied {{b13}} — typically dwarf the electricity it will sip over its working life. For such goods, the single most consequential green act is not switching them off more diligently; it is keeping them {{b14}}. A phone kept for five years is, in carbon terms, roughly twice as {{b15}} as the same phone replaced every two, however efficient the newer model claims to be.

This inverts a comfortable story we like to tell. We are encouraged to upgrade in the name of {{b16}}: the new fridge uses less power, the new car emits less, so surely replacing the old one is the responsible choice. Sometimes it genuinely is — for a device used constantly, where running emissions dominate, early replacement can pay its carbon {{b17}}. But for a great many possessions the debt is never repaid, because the bulk of the harm was done in the {{b18}}, before the thing was ever switched on. The price tag, which records only the moment of purchase, says nothing of this. It is silent about the river dammed, the ore extracted, the furnace fired half a world away.

None of this is an argument for hoarding broken junk or refusing all that is new; that would be a {{b19}}. The point is narrower and sharper. A culture that measures environmental virtue chiefly at the point of use is measuring the wrong end of the object's life. Were we to price the embodied cost honestly — to make the maker, not merely the user, accountable for the carbon already spent — the case for {{b20}} would cease to be a hobbyist's eccentricity and become plain economics. Some governments have begun to grope towards this: France now requires a repairability score on electronics, and the European Union has edged towards a 'right to repair'. These are first steps on a long road.

Ingrid, meanwhile, snaps the toaster shut and hands it back. The owner is delighted; the manufacturer, had it known, would have shrugged at a sale it never made. What the repair café offers is not nostalgia but a quiet correction to our accounting — a reminder that the greenest object is very often the one you already own, and that the cost most worth counting is the one no price tag ever shows.

blanks.explanation: 'a broken object is not necessarily rubbish'.
The maker 'would have been delighted to see replaced'.
'the reality is more diffuse and therefore harder to fix'.
'dominated not by how we use them but by how they are made'.
'early replacement can pay its carbon debt'.
'the bulk of the harm was done in the factory'.
'the case for repairability would cease to be a hobbyist's eccentricity'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay claims the most honest measure of a product's environmental cost is hidden in its making, not its use, and that durability is treated as a commercial threat. If a 'right to repair' made everything last twice as long, who would lose money — and how might an economy that no longer depends on constant replacement actually work? Sketch one change it would force.
